title: Cause and Effect: The Prophet Must Speak

heb.paragraph: The rhetorical questions in vv. 3-6 all presuppose the principle of cause and effect: every event has a cause, and every cause produces an effect. The sequence builds to the theological climax in vv. 7-8: YHWH has spoken (cause), the prophet must prophesy (effect).

God does nothing without revealing his 'secret counsel' (sod) to his servants the prophets. The term sod refers to the divine council — the heavenly assembly where God's plans are determined (cf. Jer 23:18, 22; 1 Kgs 22:19-23). The prophet's authority derives from having been admitted to this council.

panels.ctx: Chapter 3 begins the first of three 'Hear this word' speeches (3:1; 4:1; 5:1). The seven rhetorical questions (vv. 3-6) form one of the most tightly constructed arguments in prophetic literature. Each question assumes that effects have causes: lions roar because they have prey, traps spring because something triggered them. The climax (vv. 7-8) applies this logic to prophecy: the lion (YHWH) has roared, therefore the prophet must speak. Amos defends his ministry not through credentials but through logical necessity.

cross.note: 'Who has stood in the council of the LORD?' — the prophetic claim to have been admitted to the divine council, the same concept Amos invokes.
Micaiah's vision of the divine council, where God's plans are discussed and implemented through prophetic agents.
Paul's argument that God's attributes are clearly seen in creation, making humanity 'without excuse' — a cause-and-effect argument similar to Amos 3.

notes.note: 'You only have I chosen of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you.' Election brings responsibility, not privilege alone. Israel's status as chosen people makes their sin worse, not better. Greater knowledge means greater accountability.
The seven cause-and-effect questions build an irrefutable case: if every natural event has a cause, then Israel's coming judgment also has a cause — their sin.
God reveals his plans to his prophets before acting. This is both a comfort (God does not act without warning) and a burden (the prophet cannot remain silent once he has heard).

Calvin sees in this verse the foundational principle of covenant responsibility: God's election is not a guarantee of immunity but the basis for heightened accountability. The privileges of grace increase the obligations of obedience.

Calvin emphasizes the compulsion of prophetic calling: just as one cannot help fearing when a lion roars, the prophet cannot help speaking when God has spoken. This validates Amos' ministry against those who would silence him.

The verb yada ('know/choose') here carries the covenantal sense of election, as in Gen 18:19 and Hos 13:5. The 'therefore' (al-ken) introduces an unexpected consequence: election leads to judgment, not protection.

The concept of the divine 'council' (sod) appears in Ugaritic texts as the assembly of the gods. In Israelite theology, YHWH presides over this assembly, and the prophet is the earthly representative who has been granted access.

Stuart reads v. 2 as the hermeneutical key to the entire book: election means greater responsibility, not guaranteed protection. The verb yada ('know') in covenant contexts means 'to acknowledge as one's own' (cf. the suzerainty treaty formula). Israel is YHWH's covenant partner, and covenant violation demands punishment.

Stuart analyzes the seven questions as a carefully constructed logical argument moving from general observation (cause and effect in nature) to specific application (prophetic compulsion). The sequence builds in intensity: from walking together to lions catching prey to cities trembling. Each question makes the conclusion more inevitable.

Andersen and Freedman identify the rhetorical questions as a 'sorites' — a chain argument in which each premise leads to the next. They note that the questions are arranged in pairs (vv. 3-4, 5-6) with the conclusion in vv. 7-8, forming a 2+2+2 pattern. The logical structure is: just as effects presuppose causes, so prophetic speech presupposes divine revelation.

heb.paragraph: The word recurs throughout Amos (1:4, 7, 10, 12, 14; 2:2, 5; 3:9, 10, 11; 6:8). Fortresses represent military security and elite wealth. God's judgment consistently targets these symbols of power: the fire that consumes foreign fortresses will also consume Israel's.

panels.ctx: God summons witnesses to observe Samaria's corruption: the great tumult within her, the oppression among her people, those who hoard plunder and loot in their fortresses. The announcement of judgment (vv. 11-15) specifies that an enemy will strip the land, tear down fortresses, and destroy the winter and summer houses of the wealthy elite. The destruction of the ivory-decorated houses (v. 15) targets the conspicuous consumption that Amos repeatedly condemns.

cross.note: Ahab's ivory house — the precedent for the luxury Amos condemns.
The fuller description of elite luxury: ivory beds, choice food, idle music, fine wine.

notes.note: God invites foreign witnesses (Ashdod and Egypt) to observe Samaria's injustice. When pagans can recognize your sin, you have sunk very low indeed.
The shepherd's recovery of 'two leg bones or a piece of an ear' from a lion is proof of total destruction, not rescue. Only pathetic remnants will survive.
The destruction of winter and summer houses and ivory-decorated mansions targets the wealthy elite whose luxury was built on exploitation of the poor.
Calvin notes the humiliation of having pagan nations summoned as witnesses against Israel. If even Egypt — the land of oppression — can recognize injustice in Samaria, Israel's corruption is extreme.

The image of a shepherd rescuing fragments from a lion's kill is drawn from the legal provision of Exod 22:13: bringing back evidence of an animal torn by predators proves the shepherd is not liable. The 'rescue' is ironic: only worthless scraps will remain of Israel.
Archaeological excavations at Samaria have uncovered hundreds of ivory fragments from the 8th century BC, confirming the existence of the luxury industry Amos condemns.

Stuart reads the summons to Ashdod and Egypt as a forensic device: God calls expert witnesses to verify that Samaria's internal corruption is visible even to outsiders. The nations that Israel despised can see what Israel cannot — another layer of Amos' devastating irony.

Stuart interprets the shepherd metaphor (v. 12) as a legal convention: the shepherd brings back fragments to prove a predator attack (Exod 22:13). Applied to Israel, it means that the coming destruction will leave only pathetic remnants. The 'ivory houses' (v. 15) refer to rooms decorated with carved ivory inlays, confirmed by archaeology at Samaria.

Andersen and Freedman note the structural parallel between 3:9-15 and the oracles of chapters 1-2: both involve summoning witnesses, presenting charges, and announcing punishment. The Israel oracle has become a self-contained covenant lawsuit, with God as prosecutor, the nations as jury, and Israel as defendant.

themes.note: Prophecy at 9: this chapter is Amos' defense of prophetic authority. Judgment at 9: Samaria's doom is announced with forensic precision. Covenant at 8: election-for-accountability is the governing principle.

rows.label: vv.1-8
rows.text: Seven Questions: The Logic of Prophetic Compulsion

rows.label: vv.9-15
rows.text: Samaria Condemned: Fortresses and Ivory Houses

lit.note: Chapter 3 is the first of three 'Hear this word' speeches (3:1; 4:1; 5:1). It moves from the theological principle of election-for-accountability (v. 2) through the logical necessity of prophetic speech (vv. 3-8) to the specific indictment of Samaria's elite (vv. 9-15).

hebtext.note: The rhetorical questions in vv. 3-6 all presuppose the principle of cause and effect: every event has a cause, and every cause produces an effect. The sequence builds to the theological climax in vv. 7-8: YHWH has spoken (cause), the prophet must prophesy (effect).

God does nothing without revealing his 'secret counsel' (sod) to his servants the prophets. The term sod refers to the divine council — the heavenly assembly where God's plans are determined (cf. Jer 23:18, 22; 1 Kgs 22:19-23). The prophet's authority derives from having been admitted to this council.

The word recurs throughout Amos (1:4, 7, 10, 12, 14; 2:2, 5; 3:9, 10, 11; 6:8). Fortresses represent military security and elite wealth. God's judgment consistently targets these symbols of power: the fire that consumes foreign fortresses will also consume Israel's.

ppl.name: Amos
ppl.text: Amos defends his ministry not through credentials but through logical necessity…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Cause and Effect: The Prophet Must Speak

translations.text: Proclaim to the fortresses of Ashdod and to the fortresses of Egypt: “Assemble yourselves on the mountains of Samaria; see the great unrest within her and the oppression among her people.”

Proclaim to the strongholds in Ashdod and to the strongholds in the land of Egypt, and say, “Assemble yourselves on the mountains of Samaria, and see the great tumults within her, and the oppressed in her midst.”
Hear this word, people of Israel, the word the Lord has spoken against you—against the whole family I brought up out of Egypt:
Hear this word that the LORD has spoken against you, O people of Israel, against the whole family that I brought up out of the land of Egypt:
